content_markdown:
A cell is connected in series with a $2.0\,\Omega$ resistor and a switch. The voltmeter is connected across the cell and reads $12\text{V}$ when the switch is open and $8.0\text{V}$ when the switch is closed.

What is the internal resistance of the cell?

diagram_metadata.description: A circuit diagram showing a cell in series with a switch and a 2.0 ohm resistor. A voltmeter is connected in parallel across the cell.

vibe_explanation: When the switch is open, no current flows, so the voltmeter reads the electromotive force (emf) $\epsilon = 12\text{V}$. When the switch is closed, the voltmeter reads the terminal potential difference $V = 8.0\text{V}$. The current in the circuit is $I = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{8.0}{2.0} = 4.0\text{A}$. The 'lost volts' due to internal resistance $r$ is $\epsilon - V = Ir$, so $12 - 8 = 4.0 \times r$, which gives $4 = 4r$, therefore $r = 1.0\,\Omega$.
